_notes: 3-4 stars per Ro16 team. Ages as of July 2026. Clubs are each player's club at the end of the 2025-26 season; marked TODO-RESEARCH where a current club could not be verified.
tournament_stats.goals filled only where verified by the July 4 Golden Boot coverage; otherwise null with a verified note or nothing.
photo_url is filled by tools/fetch_headshots.py (Wikimedia Commons only) — see research/README.md.

players.name: Alphonso Davies
players.journey: Born in a refugee camp in Ghana to Liberian parents, raised in Edmonton, and a Champions League winner with Bayern Munich by age 19. He's the face of Canadian soccer and one of the fastest players ever timed on a pitch.
players.watch_for: A defender who suddenly turns into a winger: watch him sprint the entire left sideline in about four seconds when Canada win the ball.

players.name: Jonathan David
players.journey: Ottawa-raised and coolly lethal, David scored goals for fun in France with Lille before earning a move to Italian giants Juventus. He is Canada's all-time leading scorer while still in his mid-20s.
players.watch_for: He doesn't dribble much — he just appears unmarked in the box at the exact right moment. Watch where he is when a cross comes in.

players.name: Tajon Buchanan
players.journey: From Brampton, Ontario through American college soccer to Inter Milan and then Spain — Buchanan's route was unconventional, but his fearless dribbling made him a fixture on Canada's wing.
players.watch_for: Pure chaos on the right flank: he'll try skill moves other players wouldn't dare, and defenders never know which way he's going.

players.name: Achraf Hakimi
players.journey: Born in Madrid to Moroccan parents, Hakimi chose Morocco and became the symbol of their 2022 semifinal run. At PSG he redefined the right-back position — essentially a winger who also defends.
players.watch_for: He takes penalties with a cheeky chipped 'Panenka' and attacks like a forward. Count how often Morocco's danger comes down his right side.

players.name: Brahim Díaz
players.journey: A Real Madrid playmaker who could have played for Spain but chose Morocco, his grandfather's country, in 2024. Small, slippery and inventive — he's the creative spark between Morocco's disciplined lines.
players.watch_for: Tight-space wizardry: watch him receive the ball surrounded by three defenders and somehow glide out with it.

players.name: Yassine Bounou
players.journey: 'Bono' became a global name in 2022, stonewalling Spain in the shootout that sent Morocco to history. Calm, smiling, and huge in the biggest moments — he's the safety net under everything Morocco do.
players.watch_for: Penalty situations. He gets in takers' heads, waits until the last millisecond, and has now helped win shootouts at consecutive World Cups.

tournament_stats.note: Saved in the Round-of-32 shootout win over the Netherlands.

players.name: Julio Enciso
players.journey: Paraguay's brightest talent in a generation, a fearless attacker who left for Europe as a teenager. He announced himself to the world this week with the goal that helped topple Germany.
players.watch_for: He shoots from anywhere, without warning — if he gets the ball 25 yards out and looks up, hold your breath.

tournament_stats.note: Scored the opener in the Round-of-32 upset of Germany.

players.name: Miguel Almirón
players.journey: The heartbeat of this Paraguay side for a decade, from an MLS Cup with Atlanta to Premier League years at Newcastle and back. Endless running, endless smiling — and a rocket of a left foot.
players.watch_for: The counterattack outlet: when Paraguay clear the ball, it's usually Almirón chasing it down the wing at full sprint.

players.name: Gustavo Gómez
players.journey: Paraguay's captain and a legend at Brazilian giants Palmeiras, where he's won everything South American club football offers. The embodiment of Paraguay's identity: heads it, blocks it, means it.
players.watch_for: Watch him organize: pointing, shouting, dragging teammates into position before every opposition free kick and corner.

players.name: Kylian Mbappé
players.journey: A World Cup winner at 19, a hat-trick scorer in the 2022 final at 23, and now Real Madrid's main man at 27. He is the fastest superstar in the sport and plays every World Cup like it belongs to him.
players.watch_for: The standing start: he idles near the left touchline, then goes 0-to-full-sprint the instant a pass is played behind the defense.

tournament_stats.note: Second in the Golden Boot race after a brace against Sweden.

players.name: Ousmane Dembélé
players.journey: Once the sport's great 'what if', Dembélé rebuilt himself at PSG into the best player in the world — reigning Ballon d'Or winner and No. 1 on the Guardian's 2025 top-100 list.
players.watch_for: He's genuinely two-footed — defenders can't show him onto his 'weak' side because he doesn't have one. Watch him shoot with either foot without breaking stride.

tournament_stats.note: Among the leading scorers in the Golden Boot chase.

players.name: Michael Olise
players.journey: London-born, France-chosen, and Bayern Munich's silkiest attacker. Olise plays at his own languid tempo — until the killer pass or curling finish appears out of nowhere.
players.watch_for: The left-footed curler from the right wing: he cuts inside and bends shots toward the far corner, over and over.

players.name: Vinícius Júnior
players.journey: From the favelas of Rio to Champions League-winning goals for Real Madrid, Vinícius carries Brazil's flair tradition — the dancing feet, the celebrations, and the burden of a nation that expects joy.
players.watch_for: The one-on-one: when he lines up a fullback on the left wing, something is about to happen — a burst past him or a foul. Usually one of the two.

tournament_stats.note: In the thick of the Golden Boot race.

players.name: Raphinha
players.journey: A late bloomer who fought his way from Brazil's lower leagues through Portugal and Leeds to become Barcelona's captain-in-spirit and one of the world's most productive attackers.
players.watch_for: Corners and crossing: his deliveries are vicious, curling toward goal — even his corners are shots in disguise.

players.name: Rodrygo
players.journey: The quiet one in Real Madrid's superstar attack, with a habit of scoring enormous goals in enormous games. Smooth where Vinícius is explosive — together they've terrorized Europe for years.
players.watch_for: Big-moment radar: he drifts through games, then produces exactly the right touch in the decisive minute. Don't look away late.

players.name: Erling Haaland
players.journey: A goal-scoring cyborg from Bryne who broke the Premier League's scoring record in his first season at Manchester City. This is his first World Cup — Norway hadn't qualified since before he was born.
players.watch_for: He might touch the ball 20 times all game and still score twice. Watch his darting runs the moment a teammate's head comes up to cross.

tournament_stats.note: Two behind Messi in the Golden Boot race.

players.name: Martin Ødegaard
players.journey: A prodigy signed by Real Madrid at 16 who grew into Arsenal's captain and one of the game's most elegant passers. He and Haaland waited their whole careers for Norway to reach this stage.
players.watch_for: The disguised pass: he looks one way, opens his body the other, and slips the ball through a gap nobody else saw.

players.name: Antonio Nusa
players.journey: Norway's third star is the fun one: a fearless 21-year-old dribbler at RB Leipzig who gives this team an unpredictable edge beyond the Haaland-Ødegaard axis.
players.watch_for: He runs directly at defenders with the ball glued to his left foot — the crowd noise rises every time he gets it.

players.name: Santiago Giménez
players.journey: Son of an Argentine international, 'Santi' chose Mexico and shot his way from Cruz Azul to Feyenoord to AC Milan. He carries the No. 9 hopes of a country that has waited decades for a striker like him.
players.watch_for: Near-post runs: he attacks the front of the goal before defenders react, finishing crosses with one touch.

players.name: Edson Álvarez
players.journey: Mexico's captain and enforcer, hardened by years in the Premier League with West Ham. Every Mexican attack starts with him winning the ball; every opposition attack has to go through him first.
players.watch_for: The destroyer's art: interceptions, blocks, and perfectly-timed tackles right in front of Mexico's defense.

players.name: Gilberto Mora
players.journey: A 17-year-old playing a home World Cup — Mora became the youngest star of Mexican football at Tijuana and plays with a veteran's calm. The country has already fallen in love.
players.watch_for: How much the teenager wants the ball: he keeps demanding it between the lines in moments when grown internationals hide.

players.name: Harry Kane
players.journey: England's captain and all-time leading scorer, who finally started collecting trophies after moving to Bayern Munich. Won the 2018 World Cup Golden Boot; the only thing missing is winning the tournament itself.
players.watch_for: The false-nine drop: England's striker keeps appearing in midfield to spray passes, then arrives late in the box to finish the move he started.

tournament_stats.note: Two behind Messi in the Golden Boot race.

players.name: Jude Bellingham
players.journey: From Birmingham to Borussia Dortmund at 17 to Real Madrid galáctico at 20. Bellingham plays with a swagger England haven't produced in generations — and the country expects him to drag them to a title.
players.watch_for: The late surge into the box: he starts moves as a midfielder and finishes them as a striker, arriving exactly when the ball does.

players.name: Bukayo Saka
players.journey: London's favorite son grew from the teenager who missed a Euro 2020 final penalty into Arsenal's talisman and England's most reliable attacker. Universally adored, relentlessly productive.
players.watch_for: The same move, unstoppable anyway: receive wide right, jink inside onto the left foot, curl for the far corner.

players.name: Christian Pulisic
players.journey: 'Captain America' has carried US soccer since he was a teenager at Borussia Dortmund — a Champions League winner with Chelsea, now AC Milan's star. A home World Cup is the moment his whole career pointed toward.
players.watch_for: Direct running at the defense's weak side — and set-piece deliveries. When the US need a moment, they give him the ball.

players.name: Folarin Balogun
players.journey: Raised in London and New York, developed at Arsenal, and recruited to lead the US line. His movement and finishing gave the USMNT the true No. 9 it lacked for decades.
players.watch_for: Suspended for the Belgium match after a controversial red card against Bosnia — his absence, and who replaces him, is the American storyline of the round.

tournament_stats.note: Scored vs Bosnia in the Ro32, then was sent off — suspended for the Belgium game.

players.name: Malik Tillman
players.journey: German-born with an American father, Tillman came through Bayern Munich's academy and chose the USMNT. His free kick against Bosnia — bent over the wall and in — was the American highlight of the tournament so far.
players.watch_for: Any dead ball 25-30 yards out. After that Bosnia free kick, every wall he faces will be terrified.

tournament_stats.note: Scored a spectacular free kick in the Ro32 win over Bosnia.

players.name: Weston McKennie
players.journey: Texas-raised and Juventus-hardened, McKennie is the USMNT's energy source — a box-to-box runner who wins headers, cracks jokes, and plays every game like a backyard battle.
players.watch_for: Attacking corners: he times leaps like an NFL tight end and is the USA's biggest aerial threat in both boxes.

players.name: Kevin De Bruyne
players.journey: For a decade the best passer alive, De Bruyne defined Manchester City's dynasty before moving to Napoli. At 35, this World Cup is the last ride of Belgium's golden generation — and he knows it.
players.watch_for: The whipped cross-field ball: he hits 50-yard passes, with bend, onto a sprinting teammate's foot. Nobody else at this tournament plays that pass.

players.name: Jérémy Doku
players.journey: The most electric dribbler in the Premier League, Doku gives Belgium's aging side its youth and danger. Fullbacks have nightmares about his first three steps.
players.watch_for: One-v-one entertainment: feints, stutter-steps, and explosive bursts past defenders who know exactly what's coming and still can't stop it.

players.name: Romelu Lukaku
players.journey: Belgium's all-time leading scorer, a wall of a striker who has scored everywhere from the Premier League to Serie A. Reunited with De Bruyne at Napoli — and for one last international summer.
players.watch_for: Back-to-goal power: he pins center backs, holds the ball up one-handed, and brings Belgium's runners into play.

players.name: Lamine Yamal
players.journey: A European champion at 17, Barcelona's crown jewel, and the presumed face of football's next 15 years. He turns 19 during this tournament — and he's already Spain's most dangerous player.
players.watch_for: The left foot from the right wing: everyone on the planet knows he wants to cut inside and curl it, and it keeps working.

players.name: Pedri
players.journey: The heir to Spain's great midfield tradition — Xavi and Iniesta's spiritual successor at Barcelona. Pedri doesn't run games loudly; he dissolves opponents with a thousand perfect touches.
players.watch_for: How he never loses the ball. Seriously — count his giveaways. You may still be at zero at full time.

players.name: Mikel Oyarzabal
players.journey: The captain of his hometown club Real Sociedad and Spain's man for the biggest goals — he scored the winner in the Euro 2024 final and just put two past Austria in the Round of 32.
players.watch_for: Ruthless simplicity: no tricks, just perfect timing and first-time finishes. His two against Austria were textbook striker's goals.

tournament_stats.note: Scored twice in the 3-0 Round-of-32 win over Austria; among the Golden Boot leaders.

players.name: Cristiano Ronaldo
players.journey: The most famous athlete on earth, playing a record sixth World Cup at 41. Five Ballons d'Or, a European title with Portugal, and more goals than anyone in the history of the men's international game — everything except this trophy.
players.watch_for: The penalty-box predator, final act: he doesn't chase or press anymore, but leave him unmarked for one second — as Croatia did — and it's in the net.

tournament_stats.note: Scored from the spot in the Ro32 win over Croatia.

players.name: Vitinha
players.journey: The tiny engine of PSG's Champions League-winning midfield and No. 3 on the Guardian's 2025 list of the world's best players. While Ronaldo takes the photos, Vitinha runs the team.
players.watch_for: 360-degree awareness: he receives the ball facing his own goal and somehow turns out of pressure every single time.

players.name: Rafael Leão
players.journey: AC Milan's smooth-gliding winger — all long strides and sudden violence of pace. On his day he's unplayable; Portugal need those days now more than ever.
players.watch_for: The lazy-looking stride that isn't: he seems to be jogging until you notice the defender sprinting flat-out next to him and losing ground.

players.name: Lionel Messi
players.journey: The 2022 title completed the greatest career in the sport's history — and then he came back for more. At 39, playing his club football in Miami, Messi is somehow leading this World Cup in goals in what is almost certainly his final tournament.
players.watch_for: The walking masterclass: he strolls around reading the game like a chess board, then wins it with one pass, one dribble, or one free kick.

players.name: Julián Álvarez
players.journey: 'La Araña' (the Spider) won the 2022 World Cup as Messi's tireless sidekick, then became Atlético Madrid's superstar. He does everything: presses, links, scores, sacrifices.
players.watch_for: The first press: watch how fast he closes down defenders the instant Argentina lose the ball — it sets the tone for everything.

players.name: Lautaro Martínez
players.journey: Inter Milan's captain and one of Europe's most complete strikers — a bull in the box who also scored the winner in the 2024 Copa América final.
players.watch_for: Physical duels with the last defender: he wrestles, spins, and gets his shot off in spaces that shouldn't exist.

players.name: Mohamed Salah
players.journey: The Egyptian King — Liverpool's all-time great, a multiple Premier League Golden Boot winner, and the most beloved figure in Egyptian public life. This deep World Cup run is the one thing his career was missing.
players.watch_for: The counterattack: Egypt defend for ten men and the moment they win it, everything is launched toward Salah sprinting up the right.

players.name: Omar Marmoush
players.journey: Egypt's second superstar broke out with a goal spree in Germany and earned a big move to Manchester City. Salah finally has a partner who scares defenses on his own.
players.watch_for: Long-range hits: Marmoush shoots early and often from outside the box, with either foot.

players.name: Emam Ashour
players.journey: The star of Cairo giants Al Ahly and the engine of this Egypt side — he scored the header against Australia that started the country's greatest football week.
players.watch_for: Box-crashing runs from midfield: Egypt's counters end with Salah wide and Ashour arriving through the middle.

tournament_stats.note: Scored Egypt's goal in the historic Round-of-32 win over Australia.

players.name: Granit Xhaka
players.journey: Switzerland's captain and its emotional core for a decade — from Arsenal captain to Leverkusen's unbeaten champion to Premier League leadership at Sunderland. Loud, proud, and always in the middle of everything.
players.watch_for: Everything runs through him: count how many Swiss possessions start with Xhaka demanding the ball off the defense.

players.name: Breel Embolo
players.journey: Born in Cameroon and raised in Basel, Embolo is Switzerland's battering ram — and he just scored in the win over Algeria that ended 88 years of knockout misery.
players.watch_for: Channel running: he drags center backs into wide areas and bullies them for position on long balls.

tournament_stats.note: Scored in the Round-of-32 win over Algeria.

players.name: Dan Ndoye
players.journey: A late-blooming winger who starred in Bologna's fairytale season before moving to the Premier League. Scored in the Euro 2024 knockouts and again in this week's historic win over Algeria.
players.watch_for: Two-way sprinting on the right wing — he's often Switzerland's most dangerous attacker and their hardest-working defender in the same game.

tournament_stats.note: Scored in the Round-of-32 win over Algeria.

players.name: Luis Díaz
players.journey: From a tiny town in La Guajira to Liverpool hero to Bayern Munich star, 'Lucho' plays with pure joy — and he's the man Colombia give the ball to when they need something impossible.
players.watch_for: The slalom: head down, defenders bouncing off him, left foot cocked. When he starts weaving infield, get off your phone.

players.name: James Rodríguez
players.journey: The star of the 2014 World Cup — Golden Boot, goal of the tournament, global fame overnight. A decade of wandering later, James still saves his best football for Colombia's shirt.
players.watch_for: That left foot on a dead ball or a half-volley. It produced the best goal of a World Cup once; it can still bend a game.

players.name: Jhon Arias
players.journey: A late bloomer who became a South American club legend with Fluminense before moving to Europe. His early goal beat Ghana in the Round of 32 — quietly, he's been Colombia's most consistent attacker.
players.watch_for: Work between the lines: clever little movements into pockets of space, always available, always facing forward.

tournament_stats.note: Scored the winner against Ghana in the Round of 32.
